text: I don't know why we don't use biodigesters more to heat our homes, cook and heat our water with the gas it produces. it's a natural gas and would be far easier to get and why pay to get rid of something when it has many uses. the only reason i can think of is it doesn't creates revenue. one wouldn't have to pay for electric or natural gas or propane or pay water and sewage bills. this world is full of lies to keep people occupied and serving another man and obeying the thing nature provides if we just pay attention. thanks for the reminder.

author: @kadmow
text:
- the solar conversion to fuel briquette manufacture from sludge is great (or other waste to fuel sources- it solves the microplastic and artificial hormone contamination problem in effluent - while conserving the forests - by reducing domestic fuel pressures -  in marginal and arid zones.  (after combustion ash remains as a fertiliser source).

Remember that human and domestic biomass makes up something like (>) 80% of the land mammal biomass. - not insignificant in a historical context, that the globe provides for all is an amazing feat.
(then there are the oceanic and subterranean biomass(s) which just holds the global ecosystem together - thankyou algae and fungi.)

author: @OMIF1
text: I read a paper years ago about a system for raising pigs where they would flush the pig effluents into a digestor that produced methane that they used for fuel using a blue-green algae (i believe) . The algae would be harvested, dried and pelletized into high grade feed and fed back to the pigs. The algae also cleaned the water, which was then used to "flush" the pig waste and start the process all over again. The inputs into the system were water to replace water lost through evaporation, and supplemental food for the hogs. Sounded brilliant but I have no idea what happened to the idea.

author: @calvingreene90
text:
The problem with using municipal sewage as fertilizer is the heavy metal content.

Milorganite, which is made from Milwaukee sewage is great for golf courses but is unsuitable for food crops.

text: The Primary Resources

author: @danbhakta
text: We seem to have a long history of cyclic centralized dense urbanization and a de-centralized sparser "village" or more rural style of life.  I'll take a stab and say we are at peak urbanization to the point of being unsustainable.  The dense population centers will devolve quickly into survival mode.  Every man for himself.  All it would take would be an unrecoverable disruption of the supply chain of shit they need...water, food, fuel/energy.  What if for example, for any myriad of reasons, NYC is cut off.  Every road going in or out, all rails in or out are destroyed.  Millions locked inside as the govt has decided to quarantine the isolated city.  No flights in or out indefinitely.  How would that play out?  Millions upon millions of people locked out and imprisoned in a concrete jungle.  For a thought experiment, assume no help from outside...how quickly will the population decline, and how long before the concrete NYC landscape is transformed into agricultural land?
